questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the structure of an atom
questions.question: A learner uses a classroom atom model to check the key physics idea. Which statement about the structure of an atom is correct?
options: Electrons are found inside protons in the nucleus.
Neutrons have a positive charge.
The nucleus contains only electrons.
Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.correctAnswer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.
questions.senNote: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the structure of an atom
questions.question: A learner uses a revision diagram of a lithium atom to check the key physics idea. Which statement about the structure of an atom is correct?
options: Neutrons have a positive charge.
The nucleus contains only electrons.
Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
Protons are found in shells around the nucleus.
questions.correctAnswer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.
questions.senNote: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the structure of an atom
questions.question: A learner uses a student note about nuclei and shells to check the key physics idea. Which statement about the structure of an atom is correct?
options: The nucleus contains only electrons.
Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
Protons are found in shells around the nucleus.
Neutrons move around the outside of the atom.
questions.correctAnswer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.
questions.senNote: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the structure of an atom

options: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.

Neutrons move around the outside of the atom.
Electrons have the same positive charge as protons.
questions.correctAnswer: Protons and neutrons are found in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.
questions.senNote: The nucleus contains protons and neutrons. Electrons move around the nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a Geiger counter demonstration to check the key physics idea. What is background radiation?
options: Radiation that means an object has become radioactive forever.
Radiation produced only in nuclear power stations.
Radiation that exists only inside medical equipment.
The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
questions.correctAnswer: The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
questions.explanation: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.
questions.senNote: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.

questions.subject: Physics

options: Radiation produced only in nuclear power stations.

The low level of radiation that is always present in the environment.
Radiation that can only be detected during a nuclear accident.

questions.explanation: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.
questions.senNote: Background radiation comes from natural and artificial sources around us.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a nuclear power station diagram to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: The nucleus loses all of its protons but stays the same element.
A magnetic field splits the electrons into smaller particles.
A small nucleus joins another small nucleus at low temperature without releasing energy.
A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a star-energy explanation to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: A magnetic field splits the electrons into smaller particles.
A small nucleus joins another small nucleus at low temperature without releasing energy.
A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
Electrons split out of atoms and cause chemical burning.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a reactor fuel description to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: A small nucleus joins another small nucleus at low temperature without releasing energy.
A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
Electrons split out of atoms and cause chemical burning.
A neutral atom turns into a charged atom only because it gains heat.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a fusion research note to check the key physics idea. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct?
options: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
Electrons split out of atoms and cause chemical burning.
A neutral atom turns into a charged atom only because it gains heat.
Two large nuclei join together without changing mass.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a nuclear power station diagram to prepare for an exam-style question. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: A neutral atom turns into a charged atom only because it gains heat.

A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a star-energy explanation to prepare for an exam-style question. Which statement about nuclear fission is correct? Choose the best answer.

questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

Two large nuclei join together without changing mass.
A nucleus absorbs light and turns into an electron.
A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.correctAnswer: A large nucleus splits into smaller nuclei and releases energy.
questions.explanation: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.
questions.senNote: In nuclear fission, a large unstable nucleus splits and releases energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a radiographer safety poster to check the key physics idea. Which statement correctly compares contamination and irradiation?
options: Both words mean the same thing in radiation safety.
Irradiation always leaves radioactive atoms inside the object.
Contamination is exposure to radiation only, with no material transfer.
Contamination means unwanted radioactive material is on or in an object, but irradiation means exposure to radiation only.
questions.correctAnswer: Contamination means unwanted radioactive material is on or in an object, but irradiation means exposure to radiation only.
questions.explanation: Irradiation does not make an object radioactive. Contamination involves radioactive substances being present on or in the object.
questions.senNote: Irradiation does not make an object radioactive. Contamination involves radioactive substances being present on or in the object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a sealed-source demonstration to check the key physics idea. Which statement correctly compares contamination and irradiation?
options: Irradiation always leaves radioactive atoms inside the object.
Contamination is exposure to radiation only, with no material transfer.
Contamination means unwanted radioactive material is on or in an object, but irradiation means exposure to radiation only.
Irradiation can be washed off the skin like dust.
questions.correctAnswer: Contamination means unwanted radioactive material is on or in an object, but irradiation means exposure to radiation only.
questions.explanation: Irradiation does not make an object radioactive. Contamination involves radioactive substances being present on or in the object.
questions.senNote: Irradiation does not make an object radioactive. Contamination involves radioactive substances being present on or in the object.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the resistance of a filament lamp
questions.question: A learner uses a filament lamp graph to check the key physics idea. What happens to the resistance of a filament lamp when the current through it increases?
options: Its resistance falls to zero because the lamp glows.
Its resistance stays constant because it is ohmic.
Its resistance changes only if the potential difference is zero.
Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
questions.correctAnswer: Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
questions.explanation: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.
questions.senNote: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.

questions.question: A learner uses a current sensor trace to prepare for an exam-style question. What happens to the resistance of a filament lamp when the current through it increases?
options: Its resistance stays constant because it is ohmic.
Its resistance changes only if the potential difference is zero.
Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
Its resistance falls to zero because the lamp glows.
questions.correctAnswer: Its resistance increases because the filament gets hotter.
questions.explanation: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.
questions.senNote: As the filament lamp gets hotter, its resistance increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain the purpose of a fuse
questions.question: A learner uses a three-pin plug diagram to check the key physics idea. Why is a fuse placed in the live wire of a plug?
options: It increases the current to start the appliance.
It stores charge so the appliance works longer.
It keeps the neutral wire at a higher potential difference.
It melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes too large.
questions.correctAnswer: It melts and breaks the circuit if the current becomes too large.
questions.explanation: A fuse is a safety device. It melts if the current is too large and cuts off the supply.
questions.senNote: A fuse is a safety device. It melts if the current is too large and cuts off the supply.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a household energy bill to check the key physics idea. A 2 kW heater runs for 3 hours. Electricity costs 25 p per kWh. What is the cost?
questions.explanation: Cost equals power in kW multiplied by time in hours multiplied by the unit rate.
questions.senNote: Cost equals power in kW multiplied by time in hours multiplied by the unit rate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a portable heater use case to check the key physics idea. A 3 kW heater runs for 4 hours. Electricity costs 26 p per kWh. What is the cost?
questions.explanation: Cost equals power in kW multiplied by time in hours multiplied by the unit rate.
questions.senNote: Cost equals power in kW multiplied by time in hours multiplied by the unit rate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recall potential difference in a parallel circuit
questions.question: A learner uses a parallel lamp circuit to check the key physics idea. Which statement about potential difference in a parallel circuit is correct?
options: The current is the same in each branch because the potential difference changes.
The potential difference is shared equally only in a series circuit.
The supply potential difference becomes zero in each branch.
The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
questions.correctAnswer: The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.
questions.senNote: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recall potential difference in a parallel circuit
questions.question: A learner uses a two-branch circuit diagram to check the key physics idea. Which statement about potential difference in a parallel circuit is correct?
options: The potential difference is shared equally only in a series circuit.
The supply potential difference becomes zero in each branch.
The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
The current is the same in each branch because the potential difference changes.
questions.correctAnswer: The potential difference across each branch is the same as the supply.
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.
questions.senNote: In a parallel circuit, each branch gets the full supply potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a energy-transfer machine to check the key physics idea. A machine gives 45 J of useful energy output from 70 J of total energy input. What is its efficiency?
questions.explanation: Efficiency equals useful energy output divided by total energy input, then multiplied by 100%.
questions.senNote: Efficiency equals useful energy output divided by total energy input, then multiplied by 100%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a renewable energy debate to check the key physics idea. Which choice gives a good reason for using a renewable energy resource in the future?
options: It always gives the same power output.
It never needs any equipment.
It can be used without any cost at all.
It is less likely to run out.
questions.correctAnswer: It is less likely to run out.
questions.explanation: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.
questions.senNote: Renewable resources are replenished naturally, so they are less likely to run out.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a road safety lesson to check the key physics idea. A car travels on tired driver. Which answer is best?
options: The thinking distance becomes zero because the driver is careful.
The braking distance decreases because the car is lighter.
The braking distance stays the same because speed is unchanged.
The thinking distance increases because reactions are slower.
questions.correctAnswer: The thinking distance increases because reactions are slower.
questions.explanation: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.
questions.senNote: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a driver reaction scenario to check the key physics idea. A car travels on worn tyres. Which answer is best?

The braking distance increases because grip is reduced.
The thinking distance becomes zero because the driver is careful.

questions.explanation: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.
questions.senNote: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.

questions.question: A learner uses a braking distance discussion to prepare for an exam-style question. A car travels on worn tyres. Which answer is best?
options: The braking distance decreases because the car is lighter.
The braking distance stays the same because speed is unchanged.
The braking distance increases because grip is reduced.
The thinking distance becomes zero because the driver is careful.
questions.correctAnswer: The braking distance increases because grip is reduced.
questions.explanation: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.
questions.senNote: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a road safety lesson to test a common misconception. A car travels on icy road. Which answer is best?
options: The braking distance stays the same because speed is unchanged.
The braking distance increases because friction is much lower.
The thinking distance becomes zero because the driver is careful.
The braking distance decreases because the car is lighter.
questions.correctAnswer: The braking distance increases because friction is much lower.
questions.explanation: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.
questions.senNote: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a tyre-grip comparison to test a common misconception. A car travels on tired driver. Which answer is best? Choose the best answer.
options: The thinking distance becomes zero because the driver is careful.
The braking distance decreases because the car is lighter.
The braking distance stays the same because speed is unchanged.
The thinking distance increases because reactions are slower.
questions.correctAnswer: The thinking distance increases because reactions are slower.
questions.explanation: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.
questions.senNote: Stopping distance depends on thinking distance and braking distance. Grip and reaction time both matter.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a loudspeaker cross-section to check the key physics idea. Why does a loudspeaker cone move backwards and forwards?
options: The permanent magnet becomes hotter and pushes the cone outwards only.
The cone stores charge and releases it mechanically.
The wire stops producing a magnetic field whenever sound is needed.
The current in the coil changes direction, so the force on the coil also changes direction.
questions.correctAnswer: The current in the coil changes direction, so the force on the coil also changes direction.
questions.explanation: Alternating current changes direction, so the motor effect pushes the coil in opposite directions and the cone vibrates.
questions.senNote: Alternating current changes direction, so the motor effect pushes the coil in opposite directions and the cone vibrates.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a generator demonstration to check the key physics idea. What is electromagnetic induction?
options: A current is made by heating a wire until it glows.
A magnetic field is destroyed when iron is placed near it.
A conductor becomes a permanent magnet whenever a battery is removed.
A potential difference is produced when a conductor cuts through magnetic field lines.
questions.correctAnswer: A potential difference is produced when a conductor cuts through magnetic field lines.
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic induction happens when a conductor experiences a changing magnetic field.
questions.senNote: Electromagnetic induction happens when a conductor experiences a changing magnetic field.

A current is made by heating a wire until it glows.
A magnetic field is destroyed when iron is placed near it.
questions.correctAnswer: A potential difference is produced when a conductor cuts through magnetic field lines.
questions.explanation: Electromagnetic induction happens when a conductor experiences a changing magnetic field.
questions.senNote: Electromagnetic induction happens when a conductor experiences a changing magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe how to strengthen an electromagnet
questions.question: A learner uses a scrapyard electromagnet to check the key physics idea. How can the strength of an electromagnet be increased?
options: Use fewer turns of wire in the coil.
Remove the iron core from the centre.
Reduce the number of cells to lower the current.
Increase the current in the coil.
questions.correctAnswer: Increase the current in the coil.
questions.explanation: A larger current produces a stronger magnetic field around the coil.
questions.senNote: A larger current produces a stronger magnetic field around the coil.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a simple motor diagram to check the key physics idea. What causes the motor effect?
options: A stationary conductor produces no magnetic field and so creates gravity.
A magnetic field always stops current flowing in a conductor.
The conductor gains mass when current passes through it.
A current-carrying conductor experiences a force in a magnetic field.
questions.correctAnswer: A current-carrying conductor experiences a force in a magnetic field.
questions.explanation: The magnetic field around the conductor interacts with the external magnetic field and produces a force.
questions.senNote: The magnetic field around the conductor interacts with the external magnetic field and produces a force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why an iron core strengthens a solenoid
questions.question: A learner uses a solenoid field pattern to check the key physics idea. Why does adding an iron core to a solenoid make the field stronger?
options: The core stops the current from flowing through the coil.
The core removes all magnetic poles from the coil.
The core reduces the number of turns in the solenoid.
The core becomes magnetised and increases the magnetic field.
questions.correctAnswer: The core becomes magnetised and increases the magnetic field.
questions.explanation: The iron core becomes magnetised and concentrates the magnetic field.
questions.senNote: The iron core becomes magnetised and concentrates the magnetic field.

The core stops the current from flowing through the coil.
questions.correctAnswer: The core becomes magnetised and increases the magnetic field.
questions.explanation: The iron core becomes magnetised and concentrates the magnetic field.
questions.senNote: The iron core becomes magnetised and concentrates the magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe melting using the particle model
questions.question: A learner uses a melting ice particle model to check the key physics idea. What happens during melting?
options: The particles become colder as soon as the heater is turned on.
The mass of the substance decreases because particles disappear.
The particles stop moving completely until all the solid has melted.
Energy is used to weaken bonds between particles, so temperature stays constant while the substance melts.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is used to weaken bonds between particles, so temperature stays constant while the substance melts.
questions.explanation: During melting, energy goes into overcoming forces between particles rather than increasing temperature.
questions.senNote: During melting, energy goes into overcoming forces between particles rather than increasing temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain the effect of reducing volume on gas pressure
questions.question: A learner uses a gas syringe compression to check the key physics idea. A gas is trapped in a syringe and compressed. What happens to the pressure?
options: The pressure falls because particles get smaller.
The pressure stays constant because mass does not change.
The pressure becomes zero because the gas cannot move.
The pressure increases because the same number of particles collide with the walls in a smaller volume.
questions.correctAnswer: The pressure increases because the same number of particles collide with the walls in a smaller volume.
questions.explanation: With less space, particles hit the container walls more often, so pressure rises.
questions.senNote: With less space, particles hit the container walls more often, so pressure rises.

The pressure increases because the same number of particles collide with the walls in a smaller volume.
The pressure falls because particles get smaller.
The pressure stays constant because mass does not change.
questions.correctAnswer: The pressure increases because the same number of particles collide with the walls in a smaller volume.
questions.explanation: With less space, particles hit the container walls more often, so pressure rises.
questions.senNote: With less space, particles hit the container walls more often, so pressure rises.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain the effect of reducing volume on gas pressure
questions.question: A learner uses a sealed container heating to review the evidence in the setup. A gas is trapped in a syringe and compressed. What happens to the pressure? Choose the best answer.

The pressure becomes zero because the gas cannot move.

questions.explanation: With less space, particles hit the container walls more often, so pressure rises.
questions.senNote: With less space, particles hit the container walls more often, so pressure rises.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why gas pressure changes with temperature
questions.question: A learner uses a particle collision explanation to review the evidence in the setup. What happens to the gas pressure in a sealed container when the temperature rises?
options: The pressure falls because the particles lose mass.
The pressure stays the same because the number of particles does not change.
The pressure becomes zero because the particles spread out.
The pressure increases because the particles move faster and hit the walls more often.
questions.correctAnswer: The pressure increases because the particles move faster and hit the walls more often.
questions.explanation: Hotter particles move faster, so collisions with the container walls are more frequent and harder.
questions.senNote: Hotter particles move faster, so collisions with the container walls are more frequent and harder.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a boiling-water graph to check the key physics idea. On a heating curve, what happens while the substance is boiling?
options: The temperature rises quickly because all the energy increases particle speed.
The mass doubles because bubbles are formed.
The particles stop moving while the liquid changes into gas.
The temperature stays constant while energy is transferred to separate the particles.
questions.correctAnswer: The temperature stays constant while energy is transferred to separate the particles.
questions.explanation: During boiling, energy is used to overcome forces between particles rather than increase temperature.
questions.senNote: During boiling, energy is used to overcome forces between particles rather than increase temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a smoke-cell observation to check the key physics idea. Why does Brownian motion happen?
options: The particles are pulled by magnetic fields in the air.
The particles are heavy so they stop moving quickly.
The particles only move when light shines on them.
Small visible particles are hit unevenly by moving molecules.
questions.correctAnswer: Small visible particles are hit unevenly by moving molecules.
questions.explanation: Brownian motion is caused by random collisions from moving molecules.
questions.senNote: Brownian motion is caused by random collisions from moving molecules.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a Earth rotation model to check the key physics idea. What causes day and night on Earth?
options: Earth moves closer to and further from the Sun.
The Moon passes in front of the Sun every 24 hours.
Cloud cover changes the amount of sunlight in space.
Earth rotates on its axis.
questions.correctAnswer: Earth rotates on its axis.
questions.explanation: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.
questions.senNote: Day and night happen because Earth spins, so different parts face the Sun at different times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why satellites stay in orbit
questions.question: A learner uses a satellite orbit diagram to check the key physics idea. Why does a satellite stay in orbit around Earth?
options: There is no gravity in space, so the satellite floats in a straight line.
Air resistance above Earth pushes the satellite around the orbit.
The satellite engine must keep firing all the time to hold the orbit.
Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.correctAnswer: Gravity provides the centripetal force needed to keep the satellite moving in a curved path.
questions.explanation: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.
questions.senNote: Gravity constantly pulls the satellite towards Earth, changing its direction and keeping it in orbit.

questions.question: A learner uses a communications satellite case to check the key physics idea. Which is a common use of geostationary satellites?
options: Studying close-up images of distant galaxies
Exploring the surface of Mars directly
Measuring the magnetic field inside Earth's core
Satellite television and communications
questions.correctAnswer: Satellite television and communications
questions.explanation: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.
questions.senNote: Geostationary satellites stay above the same point on Earth, making them useful for communications and broadcasting.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a convex lens ray diagram to check the key physics idea. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is virtual and upright.
The image is always larger than the object.
The image cannot be formed by refraction.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a speaker amplitude demonstration to check the key physics idea. Which change makes a sound louder?
options: Increasing the wavelength only
Reducing the frequency
Making the speed zero
Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

options: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
Increasing the wavelength only
Reducing the frequency
Making the speed zero
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.
questions.senNote: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a slinky wave demonstration to check the key physics idea. Which statement about transverse and longitudinal waves is correct?
options: All waves are transverse in air.
Sound waves are transverse and light waves are longitudinal.
Light waves need particles to carry them.
Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.correctAnswer: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
questions.senNote: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
